content:
# Mark Chapter 3 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in Mark Chapter 3 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 3:1 — "withered hand" vs. "crippled hand" for ἐξηραμμένην τὴν χεῖρα

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a-man having the hand having-been-withered" | Hyper-literal participial construction. |
| LOGOS | "a man was there who had a withered hand" | Formal equivalent; "withered" retained. |
| PNEUMA | "a man with a crippled hand was there" | Dynamic; "crippled" as a more immediately understood term. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "withered hand" — ἐξηραμμένη literally means "dried up/withered," and this is the traditional, accurate rendering. "Crippled" is too broad and loses the specific image.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 3:2 — "They were watching him closely" vs. "watched him like hawks"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "they-were-watching him [to see] if on the Sabbaths he-will-heal him" | Literal with implied bracket. |
| LOGOS | "They watched him closely to see whether he would heal on the Sabbath" | Formal equivalent; natural English. |
| PNEUMA | "His opponents watched him like hawks, waiting to see if he would heal on the Sabbath so they could bring charges against him" | Adds "like hawks" simile and "opponents" (not in Greek); "bring charges" for κατηγορέω. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's straightforward rendering. PNEUMA's "like hawks" is vivid but adds a simile not present in the text. "So that they might accuse him" preserves κατηγορέω without over-translating.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 3:3 — "Rise into the middle" vs. "Stand up and come forward" vs. "Stand up here in front of everyone"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Rise into the middle." | Literal for ἔγειρε εἰς τὸ μέσον. |
| LOGOS | "Rise and come forward." | Natural English equivalent. |
| PNEUMA | "Stand up here in front of everyone." | Dynamic; adds "in front of everyone" to convey the public nature. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Stand up and come forward" — captures both the rising and the movement to the center implied by εἰς τὸ μέσον, without PNEUMA's added specificity.

**Status:** Resolved. Balanced rendering.

---

## 3:5 — "with anger" vs. "burning with anger" for μετ᾽ ὀργῆς

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "with anger, being-grieved at the hardness of their heart" | Literal; singular "heart" (collective). |
| LOGOS | "with anger, grieved at the hardness of their hearts" | Formal; plural "hearts." |
| PNEUMA | "burning with anger, deeply grieved by the stubborn hardness of their hearts" | Intensified; adds "burning" and "stubborn." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "with anger, deeply grieved at the hardness of their hearts." Mark states the anger plainly (μετ᾽ ὀργῆς) — "burning" is PNEUMA's intensification. However, "deeply grieved" is warranted by the compound verb συλλυπέομαι.

**Status:** Resolved. PNEUMA's "burning" rejected; "deeply grieved" adopted from PNEUMA.

---

## 3:5 — "was restored" vs. "was completely restored" for ἀπεκατεστάθη

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "his hand was-restored" | Simple rendering. |
| LOGOS | "his hand was restored" | Same as EMET. |
| PNEUMA | "his hand was completely restored" | Adds "completely" to emphasize the totality. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "was restored" without the adverb. The verb ἀποκαθίστημι already implies full restoration through its prefix; adding "completely" is redundant.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 3:6 — "conspired" vs. "were-giving counsel" vs. "began plotting"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the Pharisees immediately with the Herodians were-giving counsel against him, how they-might-destroy him" | Literal; "giving counsel" for συμβούλιον ἐδίδουν. |
| LOGOS | "The Pharisees went out and immediately conspired with the Herodians against him, as to how they might destroy him." | "Conspired" captures the hostile intent. |
| PNEUMA | "The Pharisees walked out and immediately began plotting with the Herodians to kill Jesus." | "Plotting to kill" — more blunt; names Jesus. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "conspired with the Herodians against him, plotting how to destroy him." "Conspired" (LOGOS) captures the secretive hostility; "destroy" (ἀπολέσωσιν) is broader than "kill" and may encompass political ruin.

**Status:** Resolved. Blended LOGOS/EMET.

---

## 3:10 — "pressed forward to touch him" vs. "were-falling-upon him" vs. "pushing forward to touch him"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "they-were-falling-upon him, in-order-that they-might-touch him — as-many-as had afflictions" | Literal; ἐπιπίπτω = "fall upon." |
| LOGOS | "all who had afflictions pressed upon him to touch him" | Formal equivalent; "pressed upon." |
| PNEUMA | "everyone with a disease was pushing forward to touch him" | Dynamic; "pushing forward" and "disease" for μάστιγας. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "pressed forward to touch him" — captures the physical urgency of ἐπιπίπτω without the violent connotation of "falling upon." μάστιγας ("afflictions/scourges") is better as "afflictions" than "disease," which is too clinical.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS with minor adjustments.

---

## 3:14 — "to proclaim the message" vs. "to-proclaim" vs. "to preach"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "that he-might-send them to-proclaim" | Literal; verb only, no object specified (as in Greek). |
| LOGOS | "so that he might send them out to proclaim" | Same approach; no object added. |
| PNEUMA | "to be sent out to preach" | "Preach" for κηρύσσω; restructured. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "to be sent out to proclaim the message" — adding "the message" (as SOPHIA did) provides a natural English object while staying faithful to Mark's usage of κηρύσσω throughout the Gospel.

**Status:** Resolved. Minor addition for English clarity.

---

## 3:18 — "Cananaean" vs. "the Zealot" for Καναναῖον

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Simon the Cananaean" | Preserves the Aramaic loanword from Sinaiticus. |
| LOGOS | "Simon the Cananaean" | Same as EMET. |
| PNEUMA | "Simon the Zealot" | Translates the Aramaic into English via Luke's Greek equivalent. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Retained "Cananaean" — this is the reading of Sinaiticus (ΚΑΝΑΝΑΙΟΝ), and our methodology translates from the manuscript as written. The Aramaic origin and meaning ("zealous one") belongs in the notes, not substituted into the text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 3:19 — "handed-over" vs. "betrayed" vs. "would later betray" for παρέδωκεν

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "who also handed-over him" | Literal; preserves the neutral sense of παραδίδωμι. |
| LOGOS | "who betrayed him" | Contextual rendering with moral weight. |
| PNEUMA | "who would later betray him" | Adds temporal "would later" for narrative clarity. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "who betrayed him." The aorist παρέδωκεν here is Mark's editorial comment, and "betrayed" conveys the narrative function. PNEUMA's "would later" adds temporal distance not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 3:20 — "could not even eat" vs. "couldn't even stop to eat"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "they-are not able even bread to-eat" | Literal; includes "bread" (ἄρτον). |
| LOGOS | "they could not even eat" | Formal; omits "bread" as implied. |
| PNEUMA | "he and his disciples couldn't even stop to eat" | Adds "stop to" for emphasis; specifies subjects. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "they could not even eat" — PNEUMA's "stop to" is interpretive addition. The simple statement captures the overwhelming demand.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 3:21 — "the-ones beside him" vs. "his own family" vs. "his own family" for οἱ παρ᾽ αὐτοῦ

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the-ones beside him went-out to-seize him" | Preserves the ambiguity of the Greek idiom. |
| LOGOS | "his own family...went out to seize him" | Interprets the idiom based on context (vv. 31-35). |
| PNEUMA | "his own family...set out to take custody of him" | Same interpretation; "take custody" is more dramatic. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "his own family...went out to take hold of him." The context (vv. 31-35 confirm mother and brothers) justifies translating the idiom. "Take hold of" is more precise than "seize" (which implies violence) or "take custody" (which implies legal authority).

**Status:** Resolved. LOGOS's identification accepted; verb moderated.

---

## 3:22 — "scribes" vs. "religious scholars"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the scribes, the-ones from Jerusalem having-come-down" | Literal with participial clause. |
| LOGOS | "the scribes who came down from Jerusalem" | Formal; standard "scribes." |
| PNEUMA | "the religious scholars who had come down from Jerusalem" | "Religious scholars" for γραμματεῖς. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Retained "scribes" — consistent term used throughout the translation.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 3:24–26 — "cannot stand" vs. "will collapse" / "cannot survive" / "he's finished"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "that kingdom is not able to-stand...that house will not be-able to-stand...he-is not able to-stand but has [an] end" | Literal; preserves repeated σταθῆναι. |
| LOGOS | "that kingdom cannot stand...that house will not be able to stand...he cannot stand, but his end has come" | Formal; preserves the threefold repetition. |
| PNEUMA | "A kingdom torn apart by civil war will collapse. A household divided against itself cannot survive. If Satan is fighting against himself, he's finished—his end has come." | Dynamic; varies the vocabulary for each iteration; adds "civil war." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Preserved the threefold "cannot stand" structure (EMET/LOGOS). Mark's repetition is deliberate rhetorical escalation. PNEUMA's variation ("collapse"/"survive"/"finished") loses the hammering effect of the repeated verb.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 3:27 — "plunder his goods" vs. "steal his possessions"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "to-plunder his goods...he-will-plunder his house" | Literal; preserves διαρπάσαι/διαρπάσει. |
| LOGOS | "plunder his goods...plunder his house" | Same term; formal. |
| PNEUMA | "steal his possessions...ransack his house" | Dynamic; varies verbs for English variety. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "plunder his goods...plunder his house" — preserving the repeated verb (EMET/LOGOS) maintains the parallel structure.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 3:28–29 — "blasphemies" vs. "slander"; "blasphemes" vs. "slanders"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the blasphemies, whatever they-may-blaspheme...whoever blasphemes against the Holy Spirit" | Literal; retains the βλασφημέω word group. |
| LOGOS | "whatever blasphemies they utter...whoever blasphemes against the Holy Spirit" | Formal; same approach. |
| PNEUMA | "every slander can be forgiven...whoever slanders the Holy Spirit" | Dynamic; "slander" for βλασφημέω. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Retained "blasphemies" and "blasphemes" — this is a theologically loaded term with no exact synonym. "Slander" is a legitimate meaning of βλασφημέω in some contexts, but here the theological weight of speaking against God's Spirit demands the traditional rendering.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 3:29 — "never has forgiveness" vs. "will never be forgiven" — structural difference

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "has no forgiveness unto the age, but is guilty of-an-eternal sin" | Literal; "unto the age" for εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα. |
| LOGOS | "never has forgiveness, but is guilty of an eternal sin" | Formal; "never" for the idiom. |
| PNEUMA | "will never be forgiven—that person carries the guilt of an eternal sin" | Dynamic; passive construction; "carries the guilt" expands. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering — "never has forgiveness" is faithful and direct. PNEUMA's "carries the guilt" over-explains what "is guilty of" already conveys.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 3:35 — "Whoever does the will of God—this one is my brother, and sister, and mother" vs. "Anyone who does what God wants"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Whoever does the will of God, this-one is my brother and sister and mother." | Literal with demonstrative pronoun. |
| LOGOS | "Whoever does the will of God—that one is my brother and sister and mother." | Formal; "that one" for οὗτος. |
| PNEUMA | "Anyone who does what God wants is my brother, my sister, and my mother." | Dynamic; "what God wants" for τὸ θέλημα τοῦ θεοῦ; adds possessive "my" before each. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Whoever does the will of God—this one is my brother, and sister, and mother." Preserved "the will of God" (a theologically significant phrase) and the demonstrative pronoun construction that creates emphasis.

**Status:** Resolved. Blended EMET/LOGOS.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Occurrences | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| **PNEUMA's intensifying additions** ("burning with anger," "like hawks," "completely restored") | vv. 2, 5 (twice) | Rejected; Mark's restraint preserved |
| **Vocabulary: technical/traditional vs. accessible** (scribes/religious scholars, Cananaean/Zealot, blasphemy/slander) | vv. 18, 22, 28-29 | Traditional terms retained for theological precision |
| **Rhetorical structure preservation** (threefold "cannot stand," repeated "plunder") | vv. 24-26, 27 | EMET/LOGOS repetition preserved over PNEUMA's variation |
| **Greek idiom translation** (οἱ παρ᾽ αὐτοῦ → "his own family") | v. 21 | Translated when context is unambiguous |
| **EMET's hyper-literal constructions** | vv. 1, 3, 10 | Smoothed to natural English |
| **PNEUMA's narrative additions** ("would later," "in front of everyone") | vv. 3, 19 | Generally not adopted |

**Overall Pattern Analysis:** Mark 3 shows a consistent pattern where PNEUMA's dynamic translation strategy — intensifying adjectives, adding similes, varying repeated vocabulary — often diverges from the text's own register. Mark 3 is a chapter of deliberate restraint and repetition (the threefold "cannot stand," the simple "with anger"), and the consensus preserves that restraint. EMET's structural transparency is valuable for identifying Greek patterns but rarely produces the final rendering. LOGOS again provides the most frequently adopted base text, with SOPHIA making minor adjustments for naturalness and theological precision.
